section.title: John's Testimony and the First Disciples
section.sectionRationale: After the prologue, the Gospel's narrative begins with testimony. John the Baptist tells the delegation from Jerusalem who he is not and who he is, a voice pointing away from himself to One already standing among them. He names Jesus the Lamb of God. Then, over a handful of days, the first disciples are drawn in, one bringing another, and a cascade of titles piles up: Rabbi, Messiah, Son of God, King of Israel, Son of Man.
section.thesis: The forerunner points away from himself to Jesus, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, and as the first disciples come and see, they discover Him to be the Messiah, the Son of God, and the Son of Man on whom heaven itself is opened.
section.frameNote: Four movements: John's answer to the delegation, I am not the Christ but a voice; John's declaration, Look, the Lamb of God; the first two disciples who come and see and bring others; and the calling of Philip and Nathanael, ending with the promise of an open heaven.

units.label: The witness before the delegation
units.note: I am not the Christ, but a voice

verses.text: And this was John’s {{martyria|testimony}} when the Jews of Jerusalem sent priests and Levites to ask him, “Who are you?”
He did not refuse to confess, but openly declared, “I am not the Christ.”
“Then who are you?” they inquired. “Are you Elijah?” He said, “I am not.” “Are you the Prophet?” He answered, “No.”
So they said to him, “Who are you? We need an answer for those who sent us. What do you say about yourself?”
John replied in the words of Isaiah the prophet: “I am a voice of one calling in the wilderness, ‘Make straight the way for the Lord.’”
Then the Pharisees who had been sent
asked him, “Why then do you baptize, if you are not the Christ, nor Elijah, nor the Prophet?”
“I baptize with water,” John replied, “but among you stands One you do not know.
He is the One who comes after me, the straps of whose sandals I am not worthy to untie.”
All this happened at Bethany beyond the Jordan, where John was baptizing.

units.label: Behold, the Lamb of God
units.note: who takes away the sin of the world

verses.text: The next day John saw Jesus coming toward him and said, “Look, the {{amnos|Lamb of God}}, who takes away the sin of the world!
This is He of whom I said, ‘A man who comes after me has surpassed me because He was before me.’
I myself did not know Him, but the reason I came baptizing with water was that He might be revealed to Israel.”
Then John testified, “I saw the Spirit descending from heaven like a dove and resting on Him.
I myself did not know Him, but the One who sent me to baptize with water told me, ‘The man on whom you see the Spirit descend and rest is He who will baptize with the Holy Spirit.’
I have seen and testified that this is the Son of God.”

units.label: Come and see
units.note: the first disciples follow and bring others

verses.text: The next day John was there again with two of his disciples.
When he saw Jesus walking by, he said, “Look, the Lamb of God!”
And when the two disciples heard him say this, they followed Jesus.
Jesus turned and saw them following. “What do you want?” He asked. They said to Him, “Rabbi” (which means Teacher), “where are You staying?”
“Come and see,” He replied. So they went and saw where He was staying, and spent that day with Him. It was about the tenth hour.
Andrew, Simon Peter’s brother, was one of the two who heard John’s testimony and followed Jesus.
He first found his brother Simon and told him, “We have found the {{Messias|Messiah}}” (which is translated as Christ).
Andrew brought him to Jesus, who looked at him and said, “You are Simon son of John. You will be called Cephas” (which is translated as Peter).
The next day Jesus decided to set out for Galilee. Finding Philip, He told him, “Follow Me.”
Now Philip was from Bethsaida, the same town as Andrew and Peter.
Philip found Nathanael and told him, “We have found the One Moses wrote about in the Law, the One the prophets foretold, Jesus of Nazareth, the son of Joseph.”
“Can anything good come from Nazareth?” Nathanael asked. “Come and see,” said Philip.
When Jesus saw Nathanael approaching, He said of him, “Here is a true Israelite, in whom there is no deceit.”
“How do You know me?” Nathanael asked. Jesus replied, “Before Philip called you, I saw you under the fig tree.”
“Rabbi,” Nathanael answered, “You are the Son of God! You are the King of Israel!”
Jesus said to him, “Do you believe just because I told you I saw you under the fig tree? You will see greater things than these.”
Then He declared, “Truly, truly, I tell you, you will all see heaven open and the angels of God ascending and descending on the {{huiosanthropou|Son of Man}}.”

martyria.gloss: Testimony, witness, evidence given. Witness is a major theme in John; the Baptist, the works, the Scriptures, the Father, and the Spirit all bear testimony to Jesus. John's own testimony is a model of true witness: it does not draw attention to itself but points wholly to Christ. The related word gives us the term martyr.

amnos.gloss: Lamb. The Baptist calls Jesus the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, gathering up the Passover lamb whose blood shielded Israel and the lamb led to slaughter in Isaiah 53. It announces from the start that this Messiah has come to die as a sacrifice, bearing away sin, a theme the Gospel drives toward the cross at Passover.

Messias.gloss: Messiah, the Anointed One, transliterated from Hebrew and rendered in Greek as Christos, Christ. It names the long-promised king from David's line whom God would send to rescue and rule His people. Andrew's cry, we have found the Messiah, announces that the age of promise has arrived in Jesus.

huiosanthropou.gloss: Son of Man, Jesus' favored self-designation, drawn from Daniel 7, where one like a son of man is given everlasting dominion and comes on the clouds. In John the title also marks the one who descends and ascends, the meeting point of heaven and earth. The promise of an open heaven over the Son of Man recalls Jacob's ladder, Genesis 28.

basic.context: The story proper opens with John the Baptist under questioning from a delegation of priests and Levites. He clears the ground by saying who he is not, not the Christ, not Elijah, not the Prophet, and who he is, only a voice preparing the way. Then he points to Jesus and calls Him the Lamb of God who takes away the world's sin. Over the next few days the first disciples come to Jesus, and each one brings someone else: Andrew brings Simon, Philip brings Nathanael. The passage ends with Jesus promising an open heaven.
basic.hermeneutics: Watch two things. First, the pattern of true witness: John constantly deflects attention from himself to Christ, a model for every believer. Second, the piling up of titles, Lamb of God, Rabbi, Messiah, Son of God, King of Israel, Son of Man. The key question is whether we will do what the first disciples did, come and see, and follow. The invitation come and see is how faith begins.
basic.originalLanguages: The section turns on witness, martyria, John's testimony that points only to Jesus. The great title is the amnos, the Lamb of God, announcing a sacrifice for sin. Andrew announces the Messias, the Messiah, the promised King. And Jesus crowns the titles with Son of Man, huios tou anthropou, over whom heaven is opened.
basic.typology: The Lamb of God gathers up the Passover lamb and the suffering servant of Isaiah 53. The open heaven and the angels ascending and descending on the Son of Man recall Jacob's ladder at Bethel; Jesus Himself is now the stairway between heaven and earth.
basic.christ: Every title in the passage lands on Jesus. He is the Lamb who bears away sin, the Messiah long promised, the Son of God, the King of Israel, and the Son of Man who joins heaven and earth. The forerunner's whole ministry exists to point to Him, and the first disciples' whole future begins by following Him.
basic.god: God is the one who sends: He sends John to bear witness, He reveals the sign of the Spirit descending, and He gives the promised Messiah. He is a God who initiates, seeking and calling people, and who keeps the promises of the prophets in sending His Lamb and King.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 40 supplies the voice in the wilderness. Isaiah 53 stands behind the Lamb who bears sin. Genesis 28 lies behind the open heaven and the angels on the Son of Man. Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man His dominion.
basic.oneStory: The promises are converging. The forerunner announced by Isaiah appears, points to the Lamb foreshadowed at Passover and in Isaiah 53, and the first followers recognize the Messiah of David's line. Jesus reveals Himself as the Son of Man of Daniel and the true ladder of Bethel, the one in whom heaven is opened to earth.

context.literary: The narrative opens with a sequence of days, the next day repeated, structuring the calling of the first disciples into an unfolding week. It is built on testimony and title: John's threefold denial, his declaration of the Lamb, and then a chain of personal witness, one disciple bringing another, culminating in Jesus' own self-revelation as the Son of Man. The come and see motif frames the whole.
context.historical: Messianic expectation ran high in first-century Judea, and the authorities had reason to investigate a popular baptizing prophet. Elijah was expected to return before the Day of the LORD, Malachi 4:5, and the Prophet like Moses was awaited, Deuteronomy 18:15, which is why the delegation asks precisely these questions. Bethany beyond the Jordan locates John's ministry east of the river.
context.canonical: This passage links the Old Testament forerunner prophecies to their fulfillment and introduces the titles that organize New Testament Christology. Lamb of God anticipates the Passion at Passover and Revelation's Lamb; Son of Man ties Jesus to Daniel 7 and to the ladder of Bethel, Genesis 28:12, presenting Him as the one mediator between heaven and earth.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how faith in Jesus begins, through faithful witness that points to Christ and a personal encounter that says come and see, and to introduce Jesus by the titles that reveal who He is, above all the Lamb of God and the Son of Man.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the particular calling of the first disciples in a particular week. What it prescribes and models for every age is the pattern of humble witness that points to Christ and the response of coming, seeing, following, and bringing others.

debates.question: Was John the Baptist Elijah, given that he denies it here but Jesus elsewhere links him to Elijah?

views.label: Not Elijah in person
views.case: John rightly denies being the literal Elijah returned from heaven, which is what the delegation was asking; he was not the historical prophet come back bodily.

views.label: Elijah in role and spirit
views.case: Jesus says in Matthew 11:14 and 17:12 that John came in the spirit and power of Elijah to prepare the way, fulfilling Malachi 4:5; so he is Elijah functionally though not personally.

debates.bearing: Both are true at different levels: John is not Elijah returned in person, yet he fulfills the Elijah role of preparing the Lord's way, which is why the answers only appear to conflict.

debates.question: What does Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world chiefly evoke?

views.label: The Passover lamb
views.case: John's Gospel drives toward a Passover crucifixion, and the Passover lamb's blood delivered Israel from death, so the Lamb of God is the true Passover who delivers by His blood.

views.label: The suffering servant of Isaiah 53
views.case: Isaiah 53 pictures one led like a lamb to the slaughter who bears the sin of many, matching takes away sin; the Baptist announces the servant who carries sin away.

debates.bearing: The two images reinforce rather than exclude each other: Jesus is both the Passover Lamb whose blood saves and the servant who bears sin, fulfilled at the cross.

study.originalLanguages: The passage is a study in witness. John's martyria, his testimony, is entirely deflective: he is not the Christ but only a voice preparing the way. His great word is amnos, the Lamb of God, announcing that this One has come to bear away the world's sin as a sacrifice, echoing Passover and Isaiah 53. The disciples' discovery is the Messias, the promised Anointed King, translated as Christ. And Jesus caps the titles with huios tou anthropou, the Son of Man of Daniel 7, over whom heaven stands open and the angels ascend and descend, making Him the true ladder of Bethel, the meeting of heaven and earth.

typology.type: The Passover lamb and the lamb of Isaiah 53
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world by His sacrificial death, Exodus 12 and Isaiah 53:7.

typology.type: Jacob's ladder at Bethel with angels ascending and descending
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man Himself is the stairway joining heaven and earth, over whom heaven is opened, Genesis 28:12 and John 1:51.

typology.type: The forerunner voice in the wilderness
typology.fulfillment: John the Baptist preparing the way of the Lord as Isaiah foretold, Isaiah 40:3.

study.christ: The passage names Christ from every angle. He is the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, the sacrifice foreshadowed at Passover and in Isaiah's servant. He is the One who baptizes with the Holy Spirit, greater than the forerunner who is unworthy to untie His sandal. He is the Messiah of David's line, the Son of God, the King of Israel confessed by Nathanael, and the Son of Man of Daniel 7 over whom heaven is opened. He is the true Bethel, the place where heaven and earth meet, and the goal to whom all faithful witness points.
study.god: God is the sender and initiator throughout. He sent John to bear witness, He gave the sign of the Spirit descending as a dove, and He is fulfilling the promises of Isaiah, Daniel, and the whole prophetic hope in sending His Lamb and King. He seeks people out, for it is Jesus who finds Philip and sees Nathanael under the fig tree before a word is spoken. The God of the covenant is keeping His word and gathering a people to His Son.

crossReferences.note: The voice of one calling in the wilderness, prepare the way of the LORD, which John applies to himself.
The servant led like a lamb to the slaughter who bears the sin of many, behind Lamb of God who takes away sin.
Jacob's ladder with angels ascending and descending, fulfilled in the Son of Man over whom heaven is opened.
One like a son of man given everlasting dominion, the background of Jesus' title Son of Man.

study.oneStory: The long-awaited promises begin to converge on one person. The forerunner Isaiah foretold appears and points to the Lamb foreshadowed at Passover and in Isaiah 53. The first followers recognize the Messiah of David's line, and Jesus reveals Himself as the Son of Man of Daniel and the ladder of Bethel, the one in whom heaven is opened to earth. The whole story of covenant, sacrifice, and kingship narrows here to Jesus, and the fitting response, then and now, is to come and see and follow.

quiz.q: How does John the Baptist describe himself to the delegation?

options.text: As a voice calling in the wilderness to prepare the way of the Lord, not the Christ.
options.note: Verse 23 quotes Isaiah 40:3; John points wholly away from himself to Christ.

options.text: As the prophet Elijah returned in person.
options.note: He explicitly denies being Elijah in verse 21.

options.text: As one preparing the way by baptizing with water.
options.note: True of his ministry, but his self-description here is the voice preparing the Lord's way, a role greater than the baptism itself.

options.text: As a teacher gathering his own following.
options.note: John deliberately deflects from himself; he is not building his own following but pointing to Christ.

quiz.q: What does John mean by calling Jesus the Lamb of God?

options.text: Jesus is the sacrifice who takes away the sin of the world, fulfilling the Passover lamb and Isaiah's servant.
options.note: Verse 29 ties the title to bearing away sin, evoking Passover and Isaiah 53.

options.text: Jesus is gentle and harmless like a lamb.
options.note: Gentleness is not the point; the title announces a sacrificial death that removes sin.

options.text: Jesus is a sacrifice whose blood delivers His people.
options.note: True and central, but it narrows to deliverance and omits that He takes away the sin of the world.

options.text: Jesus is simply another prophet sent to Israel.
options.note: The Lamb of God is far more than a prophet; He is the sin-bearing sacrifice.

quiz.q: How do the first disciples come to Jesus in this passage?

options.text: Through personal witness and the invitation to come and see, one bringing another.
options.note: Andrew brings Simon, Philip brings Nathanael; faith spreads by invitation to come and see.

options.text: By Jesus performing miracles that forced them to believe.
options.note: No miracle compels them here; they come through testimony and encounter.

options.text: Because John the Baptist commanded them to leave him.
options.note: John pointed to Jesus, but the disciples followed freely; he did not issue a command to leave.

options.text: By hearing John call Jesus the Lamb of God and then following.
options.note: True for the first two disciples, but the fuller pattern is personal witness and come and see spreading to others.

quiz.q: What does Jesus promise Nathanael he will see?

options.text: Heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man.
options.note: Verse 51 recalls Jacob's ladder; Jesus is the meeting of heaven and earth.

options.text: The literal fig tree miraculously bearing fruit.
options.note: The fig tree was where Jesus saw him, not the object of the promise.

options.text: Greater signs and works than the ones he has just witnessed.
options.note: Jesus does say he will see greater things, but the specific promise is the open heaven over the Son of Man.

options.text: The kingdom of Israel restored to political power.
options.note: Nathanael confessed Jesus as King of Israel, but the promise points to heaven opened, not a political restoration.

quiz.q: What titles are given to Jesus in this passage?

options.text: Lamb of God, Rabbi, Messiah, Son of God, King of Israel, and Son of Man.
options.note: All these titles appear across verses 29 to 51, revealing who Jesus is.

options.text: Only Rabbi and Teacher, ordinary titles of respect.
options.note: Those titles appear, but the passage piles up far higher titles like Lamb of God and Son of Man.

options.text: Messiah, Son of God, and King of Israel.
options.note: These are all present, but the list leaves out the crucial Lamb of God and Son of Man.

options.text: Prophet, priest, and Elijah returned.
options.note: These are not the titles given to Jesus here; Elijah is denied of John, not applied to Jesus.

questions: John the Baptist constantly pointed away from himself to Jesus. Where are you tempted to draw attention to yourself, and what would it look like to be a voice that points to Christ?
Jesus is announced as the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. How does it steady your conscience to know your sin was carried away by Him?
The disciples came because someone said come and see. Who first invited you toward Jesus, and whom might you invite now?
Jesus saw Nathanael under the fig tree before Philip called him. How does it affect you to know that Jesus already sees and knows you before you come to Him?
Andrew's first act was to find his brother Simon. Who is the one person God may be calling you to bring to Jesus?

translationNotes.note: John quotes Isaiah 40:3, a voice of one calling in the wilderness; the BSB footnote points to the passage and its Greek Old Testament form. He applies the forerunner prophecy to himself.
I baptize with water; the BSB footnote notes the preposition can also read in, here and in verses 31 and 33. The contrast is with the One who will baptize with the Holy Spirit.
This is the Son of God; the BSB footnote records a variant, the Chosen One of God, in some manuscripts. Either reading is a high confession of who Jesus is.
Cephas is Aramaic for rock, translated into Greek as Peter; the Gospel explains the name for its readers, as it does with Messiah and Rabbi.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 1:19-51. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped, embedded speaker turns joined with single spaces. The em dash in verse 45 (prophets foretold, Jesus of Nazareth) was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
